author: @PressToDigitate
text: You have a great setup there for doing a wide variety of research; I loved the lab-tour video from the day before.  You have all of the resources you need to deliver a major breakthrough in energy chemistry & configuration engineering.  Money will help, at the proper instance, but figuring out which direction you want to go is your greatest challenge.  Lexan, Post-It Notes, and even Gasoline were all accidental blockbuster success stories in Chemistry, and there have been many others.  Yours has yet to occur, but I'd bet it will involve Lightweight Kitty Litter, brought to the brink of "Incipient Wetness"...   ;-)

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Visionary, brilliant, hard-working, stubborn, humorous, friendly, patient,...
Did I forget any attribute for you, Robert?😃
You serve as an example in so many ways.👍
You talked about moving priorities from pure battery stuff to more educational ones, and I have to admit that I learned quite a lot during the last months from you, partly just beyond the threshold of consciousness. This isn't restricted to the technical details only, but also to the way of approaching a topic.🤔
At first I so often thought like "Nice idea, but unfortunately such a tiny scale, just useless for our / my requirements". (I just thought, since I prefer thinking *before* commenting.)
But you were so incredibly patient in repeating over and over again that the scale doesn't matter at this early state of development. Soon enough it hit my mind also, now just enjoying you sharing of early thoughts and ideas, using it as kick-off for own further development plans or, well, just enjoying and being fascinated.😊

On a word to your today's topic:
Even despite all of the above I'm really impressed by your honesty about the situation of and impact on your business. I didn't expect you to explain to the broad audience (although you answered to some comments already). There are not many entrepreneurs that would do so!👍

I wish you and your team from the bottom of my heart to pull through these days continuously, to reach some save haven or at least calm waters, and thundering success for your new battery!🙏🤞

Not being able to join the membership at the moment, the "regular" subscription, lots of likes, comments and watching the ads hopefully support you also, at least to some degree.😉

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely does mate - cheers - the support is tremendously appreciated

author: @philipvernejules9926
text: .........So I looked up specs for the twizy . We have nothing like it in Australia ...100 kg lithium battery , nominal range of 50 miles , two models of different top speeds . The BMS for all vehicles is becoming a huge pain . Scotty kilmer the mechanic was having a rant about it with accompanied gesticulating . Can't replace a battery on new vehicles without first using a scan tool to check through and choose some prompts otherwise a new battery doesn't last more than a year. ..To get same usable reliable energy storage from lead acid as 100 kg lithium would perhaps require 300 to 360 kg of batteries .  .Cutting through the waffle :- did you try having your capacitor battery in parallel with a smaller lithium battery to fool the BMS ? Perhaps also  a chopper circuit that connects the capacitor battery on and off to the parallel lithium battery at a tweaked sweet spot frequency if BMS ain't so readily fooled .Anyways you obviously have received heaps of advice.

replies.author: @philipvernejules9926

author: @shannonr6976
text: If you take a ring magnet from a microwave let's say. And take a vcr head. What I do is use PVC and fit it to the vcr head. Ok now at the other end of the PVC it's going to need to be cut at a angle.  Just a slight angle 22 degrees give or take not much. Ok then I tape the ring magnet to the angle of the PVC. So now there is a ring magnet that when it is  spinning . It is a eliptical rotation.  Now if you take just a regular ceramic rectangle 1.5 x 1 or really anything about that size and hold it to the ring magnet edge so it is opposed . So its edge to edge.  You will see that the ring magnet begins to spin.   This is really easy to make. I have not been successful at building something to make the rectangle magnet bounce a little.  But if that is able to be accomplished.  That is a papetuarel motion machine.  I hope I explained it right. Vcr head with a piece of PVC fitted to it. With the Slight angle at top for the 2 inch microwave ring magnet. Rectangle magnet held close to the ring magnet.  And that will cause a spin.

hey i followed your diy microwave transformer project.
hey i got a microwave transformer and i removed the primary and secondary coils.... I know you kept the primary but i had to remove both coils as i had no choice.

author: @brothernobody1775

what about using the carbon ink in the thermos as a heat source. paint a coil on the inner cylinder or something. hell, maybe add the battery into the base of the whole contraption. you're already futzing with the insulator material.

replies.author: @philipvernejules9926
replies.text: ......A thermos flask with heater is a good idea . Difficult to implement for a vacuum flask thermos, and vacuum flasks are the better performers, their weakly insulated points are the opening and is where we can feel the heat loss when they are closed up , so the other good improvement would be for the screw-on cup to be actually insulated also .  Best brands I've come across are a Tennessee make and a British make . Sadly they break easily if dropped ....I do look at Rob's coffee cup often and think he should do what I've done. I've placed a 55 watt halogen globe into a larger borosilicate glass tube ( can be sealed shut at one end in the gas flame) and fashioned it into a heater I can immerse into a cup that requires reheating .It's choice of 55or95 watts , the other low beam filament is also available .

I also recall you talking about doing something interesting with clay batteries, like trying nickel iron clay batteries, but Covid seems to have killed that too. But regardless I still love your work and your youtube channel, these are only minor things. Also don't get too hooked on the youtube train. I know of some BIG people that have been totally shutdown by youtube and had their accounts terminated suddenly & completely for violating some nonsense issue. Just warning you that youtube has a sinister side and don't become too dependent on them. Personally I have grown to loathe youtube's censorship and corrupt dealings with anyone who promotes TRUTH. One of my favourite Pastors on youtube has been totally shutdown, he had approx 140K subscribers then the next day he had zero, completely shutdown. There are some very sinister things going on atm in our world and I'm just letting you know it can all disappear overnight if youtube decides you're no longer useful for their agenda.

author: @martinbobfrank
text:
Well, I only started watching maybe four weeks ago and maybe less. I saw batteries were your thing and that intrigued me but never exactly floated my boat. But, you came across as a very knowledgable person and somebody who could actually use that information to build things and not just other ideas.
I also liked the fact that you combine electronics, chemistry and down to earth building techniques. It reminds me of the old electronic magazines which would combine tutorials and practical projects. Are they still a thing because I haven't seen any in a long time? Maybe in this throw-away society, there are not many people interested enough for magazine production. I like this combination and have just become a member for a mighty sum of £5 a month.
It's the first time I have ever become a member of any YouTube channel, because why should I pay one channel for half the price of a Netflix or Prime subscription? The answer for me was a combined number of reasons. One was your knowledge. Two, was the way you easily express it (shows your unconscious competence). Three, you put it into practical projects. Four, the people who are members must have an awesome combined amount of knowledge, and I can leach from that to learn and develop more.
It was like buying one of the old-style magazines as mentioned but on steroids. The amount of videos and tutorials is mind-blowing. Was it worth £5? Blimey yes, and unlike a magazine there is always another page to turn, and the comments to read.
Awesome. Thank you. And, I hope the battery thing works out for you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - i am certainly glad that you have found it worth it - and thanks for taking the time to post

Here's A Little Idea For You To Share Rob , Back In The 80s My Dad's Electric Meter Was Removed Because ....
Well My Cousin Gordon Has Been A Tv VCR Engineer For Years So He Came Up With This Little Unit , A Wooden Box , A 12v Kids Electric Car Motor A Single Diode , 4 Rechargeable AA Cells And And 4 AA Cell Holder He Fabricated A Winding Handle And Within 2 Minutes Of Winding Them 4 Cells Would Run My Dad's Radio And Little Torch For 20 Hours IT Was Only Primitive But It Worked Perfectly 
Bless Up Earthling

author: @aquarionh2o132
text: Global average deaths from flu on a year to year basis ranges from 250,000 to 650,000 deaths.  COVID to date has only killed 500,000 (ish) people.  So, thus far, COVID is showing itself to be no more fatal than the common annual flu.  COVID has nothing on the 1968 pandemic (H3N2 if memory serves) which killed over 1 million people.  Yet, no lockdowns took place.  No economic devastation due to the lockdowns.  No food shortages due to lockdowns (which won’t full fully realized until the Fall).  The public hype around COVID is totally silly (to be polite).  There are no documented cases of children infecting adults, and arguably most of the people who have died from COVID would have likely died from the next thing they contracted (e.g., the flu).  American CDC has continued to reduce the fatality rating of COVID from 3.4%, down to 2.0%, down to 0.40% (as of May 22nd, 2020), with the expectation that the ultimate fatality rate will likely settle out to be between 0.17% and 0.26% - far lower than the common flu.  No vaccine has ever been successfully developed for a Corona Virus (not SARS, not MERS).  Vaccines were attempted; however, the results ranged from null effect to inducing the disease causing death.  If/when a COVID19 vaccine hits the market (if it does so in under 5 years) it will be severely under tested.  The “test study” will be the general public themselves, with front line workers likely being mandated to have the untested vaccine if they wish to keep their jobs.  The only solidly objective way for the layperson to track the reality of COVID is to look at the last 10 years of annual deaths for their country and compare it to the total deaths in 2020 once the numbers are released in 2021.  Conservatively speaking, I fully expect to see no statistically significant change for any country.  The only change will be the “official” cause of death, with the annual numbers continuing on their trend of slight increase on a year to year basis as the “baby boomers” get older.  Just a voice of sanity in the insanity that has been generated over a highly contagious but otherwise relatively mild virus.   I could lay out a good deal more statistics on COVID here and how the “official numbers” are very misleading, but I’ll leave that alone for now.  If people are infested they can ask in their replies to this posting.  Cheers.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: You really should look into how the microfibrils affect multiple organs in the body and think about the long term consequences to health before you blather on that Covid 19 is not as bad as the Flu.

replies.author: @aquarionh2o132
replies.text: Barskor1 Just looking at fatalities here for starters.  To carry on there though, 98% of officially cases are mild and show little to no signs of any longer term concerns.  The 2% of sever cases needing hospitalization etc, sure, systemic effects; however, for those in that category of susceptibility any infection would have a systemic effect.  Again, COVID poses little to no fatality threat to the vast majority of the population.  All that said, no reliable long term data is available nor will it be for some time.  I fully expect COVID to be like SARS and MERS before it....a one hit wonder, and no “second wave”.  Gone & done.

replies.author: @aquarionh2o132
replies.text: I totally agree, depending on your country, yes.  But there are multiple clinical studies showing inexpensive therapies that work exceedingly well; however, these therapies are not pharmaceutically based so no doctor in Western countries can recommend or prescribe them despite their effectiveness (unless you want to lose you medical license).  The largest of these studies are 3 large clinical trials using vitamin C for the prevention, treatment, and cure of COVID.  Zero fatalities and speedy recovery, and no new contagion were the results.  So don’t kid yourself here.  A corporation’s primary mandate is to produce profit for its shareholders.  Loss of life has nothing do with profits, so profit trumps the value of public lives being lost.  On top of which the pharmaceutical industry is not designed around finding a “cure” for any disease, only the mitigation of symptoms over long periods of time (because there’s more profit in that than curing a disease).

replies.author: @aquarionh2o132
replies.text: Michael Walter Current estimates of total infected population in the USA is around 50 million, with approx 38% of the population already with immunity.  Of those infected, anyone with no or mild symptoms very seldom get tested.  The “official confirmed” cases are predominantly the more sever cases for which people have required or accepted medical assistance.  So, in sort, the official numbers are the numerator if the equation.  The denominator is the estimated 50 million infected people.  All that said, just as with any pandemic or disease the true denominator is never known (only estimated, if that).  The only way to truest know would be to test everyone in a given population for the virus and antibodies....not going to happen.

replies.author: @philipvernejules9926
replies.text: .........you can't compare oranges to apples. To get a better figure for tally of infections  I still think that multiplying the death tally by 200 gives the best guess. That'll tally then to 100 million to date

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I, for one, was glad to see you had a "paid membership" program.  I feel your channel is invaluable for my needs, and it gives me a way to "pay you" for the content I'm using.  I want to make sure I'm helping to support and sustain your efforts so that I can look forward to more of the content I need.  There aren't many times where I've actually been eager to pay "membership fees" for things, but this is certainly one of them!  I watch this more than I ever stream anything on Netflix, and you're half the price!  It's really a no-brainer.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
in many things you stated in this video I'm absolutly with you. Like you I'm very stubben as well. And I see (I think) more importance of batterys like most people: Energy storage is the main brickstone to get away from coal, oil and gas. To change over to solar / wind tecnology we need a massive amount of storage in near future because the sun does'nt shine at night and the wind blows not continously. It's not science fiction to say that E-Cars are on their way, we get more and more electrical equipment in home and factories. This to feed with energy fron focile carriers like oil, gas etc. would destroy our envroyment.
So in my mind it's important to reserch on batteries and I see it as a huge gift that there are someone like you in the world who share the results and suggestions about it.

author: @twestgard2
text:
I’m one of the people who found you after the change, but it was because I was learning to make and rehabilitate lead-acid batteries. What I appreciate most about your channel is that your videos are so well planned and edited. Certainly you could do more polishing but what I mean to highlight is that you aren’t one of those winging-it, word-vomit atrocities. 

Obviously the content is important also, but for my part at least, you’re presenting excellent material. I skip some things that don’t pique my interest. 

If there’s anything I’d like to see you do differently, I could benefit from a detailed, searchable index of your videos. YouTube doesn’t seem to support this very well because their search function “helpfully” offers a lot of lower-quality channels on whatever topic. So you’d probably have to do it on a separate website. I know that would be a ton of extra work for you but it would help those of us unwilling to scroll through endlessly here. 

Anyway, bottom line is I’m grateful for the excellent product you’re putting out. Would you post a link or something that says what the members channel is about and how to sign up?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - thanks for the idea

replies.author: @zylascope
replies.text: There is a link to the members channel in the description, below the video. Robert teaches us amazing things in the members channel. We've learnt lots of ways to make batteries, in the chemistry and the practical construction, and many other inventions including things he just discovered. Robert did a great series of videos on making/restoring lead acid batteries on the members channel that you would find useful. He goes into much more detail in the members channel than he does in the free youtube channel. I recommend it. Hope this helps.

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: zylascope Thanks!

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
Your information is helping to change the world.  Greatly appreciated.  

Do you have any videos centered on thermo generators?   With the huge amount of waste heat a cost effective method of capturing free electrons would be quite amazing.   

Bismuth telluride is pricy.  My best output has come from simple aluminum wire.  Thoughts?

replies.author: @zylascope
replies.text: Are you saying that you have  generated electricity from heat  with only aluminium wire?

replies.author: @ronaldd2154
replies.text:
@zylascope no. You'd need two dissimilar metals / bi metallic strip. Robert has experimented with his "conductive paints" years ago which he imagined you could paint different layers on your roof with to create electricity.

Edit : A guy made one with copper / copper oxide.... So wonder if aluminium / aluminium oxide would work...... 🤔

author: @Mister_Gillespie
text:
Candid and honest appraisal. This is my most coveted YT channel by a long shot. I just love it; especially the battery work. I just want the team to succeed and hope the new battery production venture comes up trumps.

In these uncertain and febrile times, we need more examples of this sort of ingenuity and open thinking to both restart the economy and provide positivity going forward.

Keep up the great, great work

Ian, Birmingham

replies.author: @AgentBenzo
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering - 
"Too many projects going on" ... 
"Like a kid in a candy store, with too many choices" ...
"All ya do is spin round 'n round - never making a decision because of alllll the glittery things"...

It's not word-for-word what you said, but the general spirit of the message .... and exactly what I needed to hear. 

Your channel is by far the most powerful channel I've ever seen out here; and the only one I have ever considered donating to ....

You've inspired me to join the members channel and hopefully help in some way.

Thanks for your dedication.
